primitives.name: Limit
primitives.formalStatement: The limit of f(x) as x approaches a is L, written lim_{x->a} f(x) = L, if for every epsilon > 0 there exists delta > 0 such that 0 < |x - a| < delta implies |f(x) - L| < epsilon.
primitives.computationalForm: Evaluate lim_{x->a} f(x): try direct substitution. If indeterminate (0/0, inf/inf), use algebraic simplification, L'Hopital's rule, or squeeze theorem.

dependencies.description: Limits are defined on the real numbers
Epsilon-delta definition uses absolute value for distance
Completeness ensures limits of convergent sequences exist in R

compositionRules.yields: Foundation for epsilon-delta analysis: the limit exists because R is complete
conditions: When proving existence of limits in the real number system
compositionRules.example: lim_{x->0} sin(x)/x = 1 (exists because R is complete and the sequence converges)

applicabilityPatterns: finding the value a function approaches near a point where it may not be defined
establishing the foundation for derivatives and integrals

primitives.name: Continuity
primitives.formalStatement: A function f is continuous at a point a if: (1) f(a) is defined, (2) lim_{x->a} f(x) exists, and (3) lim_{x->a} f(x) = f(a). f is continuous on an interval if continuous at every point in the interval.
primitives.computationalForm: Check continuity: evaluate lim_{x->a} f(x) and compare to f(a). Continuous functions: polynomials, exponentials, trig functions (on their domains). Operations preserving continuity: +, -, *, /, composition.

dependencies.description: Continuity is defined in terms of limits

compositionRules.yields: The no-surprises condition: the limit equals the function value
conditions: When checking whether a function is well-behaved at a point
compositionRules.example: f(x) = sin(x)/x at x=0: undefined, but lim = 1, so defining f(0)=1 makes it continuous

applicabilityPatterns: verifying that a function has no jumps or breaks at a point
determining the domain on which a function is well-behaved for calculus

primitives.name: Derivative
primitives.formalStatement: The derivative of f at x is f'(x) = lim_{h->0} [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h, when this limit exists. It represents the instantaneous rate of change of f at x, and the slope of the tangent line to the graph at (x, f(x)).
primitives.computationalForm: f'(x) = lim_{h->0} (f(x+h)-f(x))/h. Notation: f'(x), df/dx, Df(x). Tangent line: y = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a).

dependencies.description: The derivative is defined as a limit of the difference quotient
Differentiability implies continuity (but not conversely)

compositionRules.yields: The fundamental connection: differentiation and integration are inverse operations
conditions: When switching between rate-of-change and accumulation perspectives
compositionRules.example: d/dx integral_0^x t^2 dt = x^2 by FTC

applicabilityPatterns: computing the instantaneous rate of change of a quantity
finding the slope of a curve at a specific point

primitives.name: Differentiability
primitives.formalStatement: A function f is differentiable at a if f'(a) = lim_{h->0} [f(a+h) - f(a)]/h exists. Differentiability implies continuity. A function may be continuous but not differentiable (e.g., |x| at x=0).
primitives.computationalForm: Check differentiability: verify lim_{h->0+} [f(a+h)-f(a)]/h = lim_{h->0-} [f(a+h)-f(a)]/h. If left and right derivatives differ, f is not differentiable at a.

dependencies.description: Differentiability is the existence of the derivative
Differentiability is a stronger condition than continuity

compositionRules.yields: Hierarchy: differentiable => continuous => defined (each implication is strict)
conditions: When classifying the regularity of a function
compositionRules.example: |x| is continuous everywhere but not differentiable at x=0 (corner)

applicabilityPatterns: determining whether a function has a well-defined tangent line at a point
checking prerequisites for applying differentiation rules

primitives.name: Higher-Order Derivatives
primitives.formalStatement: The nth derivative f^(n)(x) is obtained by differentiating f n times. f''(x) = d^2f/dx^2 (acceleration, concavity). f^(n)(x) = d^n f/dx^n. A function is C^n if its first n derivatives are continuous.

dependencies.description: Higher derivatives are iterated first derivatives

compositionRules.yields: Taylor coefficients: f(x) ~ sum f^(n)(a)/n! * (x-a)^n, each coefficient from an nth derivative
conditions: When constructing polynomial approximations of functions
compositionRules.example: e^x: f^(n)(0) = 1 for all n, so Taylor series = sum x^n/n!

applicabilityPatterns: computing acceleration as the second derivative of position
determining concavity and inflection points of curves

primitives.name: Critical Point
primitives.formalStatement: A critical point of f is a value c in the domain of f where f'(c) = 0 or f'(c) does not exist. Critical points are candidates for local maxima, local minima, or inflection points.
primitives.computationalForm: Find critical points: solve f'(x) = 0. Also check where f'(x) is undefined but f(x) is defined. Classify using first or second derivative test.

dependencies.description: Critical points are defined in terms of the derivative

compositionRules.yields: Complete local extremum classification: find critical points then test sign changes
conditions: When determining whether a function has maxima or minima
compositionRules.example: f(x) = x^3 - 3x: f'(x) = 3x^2 - 3 = 0 at x = +/-1. Sign change analysis: min at x=1, max at x=-1

applicabilityPatterns: finding where a function might achieve its maximum or minimum value
locating turning points of a curve

primitives.name: Inflection Point
primitives.formalStatement: An inflection point is a point where the concavity of f changes: f'' changes sign. At an inflection point, the tangent line crosses the curve.
primitives.computationalForm: Find inflection points: solve f''(x) = 0, then verify f'' changes sign. Concave up: f'' > 0. Concave down: f'' < 0.

dependencies.description: Inflection points are where the second derivative changes sign

compositionRules.yields: Complete curve analysis: critical points for extrema, inflection points for concavity changes
conditions: When sketching or fully analyzing the shape of a function
compositionRules.example: f(x) = x^3: inflection at x=0 (f''=6x changes sign), no extrema

applicabilityPatterns: determining where a curve changes from bending upward to bending downward
identifying transition points in growth models

primitives.name: Power Rule
primitives.formalStatement: For any real number n: d/dx(x^n) = n*x^(n-1). This holds for integer, rational, and real exponents (where the function is defined).
primitives.computationalForm: d/dx(x^n) = n*x^(n-1). Examples: d/dx(x^3) = 3x^2, d/dx(sqrt(x)) = 1/(2*sqrt(x)), d/dx(1/x) = -1/x^2.

dependencies.description: Power rule is derived from the limit definition of derivative

compositionRules.yields: Generalized power rule: d/dx(f(x)^n) = n*f(x)^(n-1)*f'(x)
conditions: When differentiating composite power expressions
compositionRules.example: d/dx(sin(x))^3 = 3*sin^2(x)*cos(x)

applicabilityPatterns: differentiating polynomial terms and power functions
finding the derivative of any expression of the form x^n

primitives.name: Chain Rule
primitives.formalStatement: If g is differentiable at x and f is differentiable at g(x), then the composite function f(g(x)) is differentiable at x with: d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) * g'(x).
primitives.computationalForm: d/dx f(g(x)) = f'(g(x)) * g'(x). Leibniz notation: dy/dx = (dy/du)*(du/dx) where u = g(x). Multiple composition: d/dx f(g(h(x))) = f'(g(h(x)))*g'(h(x))*h'(x).

dependencies.description: Chain rule is a fundamental differentiation theorem

conditions: When differentiating a power of a composite function

applicabilityPatterns: differentiating compositions of functions like sin(x^2) or e^(3x)
computing rates of change through connected relationships

primitives.name: Product Rule
primitives.formalStatement: If f and g are differentiable, then d/dx[f(x)*g(x)] = f'(x)*g(x) + f(x)*g'(x).
primitives.computationalForm: (fg)' = f'g + fg'. Leibniz: d(uv) = u*dv + v*du. Extension to three functions: (fgh)' = f'gh + fg'h + fgh'.

dependencies.description: Product rule is a fundamental differentiation theorem

compositionRules.yields: Complete toolkit for differentiating rational expressions
conditions: When differentiating products and quotients of functions
compositionRules.example: d/dx[x*sin(x)] = sin(x) + x*cos(x)

applicabilityPatterns: differentiating a product of two functions
deriving integration by parts from the product rule

primitives.name: Quotient Rule
primitives.formalStatement: If f and g are differentiable and g(x) != 0, then d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [f'(x)*g(x) - f(x)*g'(x)] / [g(x)]^2.
primitives.computationalForm: (f/g)' = (f'g - fg') / g^2. Mnemonic: lo*d(hi) - hi*d(lo) over lo*lo.

dependencies.description: Quotient rule is a fundamental differentiation theorem
Quotient rule can be derived from the product rule

compositionRules.yields: Complete differentiation of rational expressions: products and quotients
conditions: When differentiating fractions of functions
compositionRules.example: d/dx[sin(x)/x] = [cos(x)*x - sin(x)] / x^2

applicabilityPatterns: differentiating a fraction where both numerator and denominator are functions of x
finding rates of change of ratios
keywords: lo d hi minus hi d lo

primitives.name: Mean Value Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: If f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), then there exists c in (a,b) such that f'(c) = [f(b) - f(a)] / (b - a). The instantaneous rate equals the average rate at some interior point.
primitives.computationalForm: Find c: solve f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a). MVT guarantees existence but may not give a unique c. Special case (Rolle's): if f(a)=f(b), then f'(c)=0 for some c in (a,b).

dependencies.description: MVT relates the derivative to the average rate of change
Continuity on [a,b] is a hypothesis of MVT
Differentiability on (a,b) is a hypothesis of MVT

compositionRules.yields: The bridge from local (derivative) to global (integral): MVT connects pointwise and average behavior
conditions: When proving that a function's average behavior is achieved at some point
compositionRules.example: A car averaging 60 mph over 2 hours must have been going exactly 60 mph at some instant

applicabilityPatterns: proving that a function achieves its average rate at some interior point
establishing bounds on function values from derivative bounds

primitives.name: L'Hopital's Rule
primitives.formalStatement: If lim_{x->a} f(x)/g(x) gives an indeterminate form 0/0 or inf/inf, and if lim_{x->a} f'(x)/g'(x) exists, then lim_{x->a} f(x)/g(x) = lim_{x->a} f'(x)/g'(x).
primitives.computationalForm: For 0/0 or inf/inf: lim f/g = lim f'/g'. May need to apply multiple times. Other indeterminate forms (0*inf, inf-inf, 0^0, 1^inf, inf^0) must be rewritten first.

dependencies.description: L'Hopital's rule evaluates limits of indeterminate quotients
The rule replaces the limit with a limit of derivatives

compositionRules.yields: Powerful limit evaluation: convert hard limits to easier ones via differentiation
conditions: When direct substitution yields 0/0 or infinity/infinity
compositionRules.example: lim_{x->0} sin(x)/x = lim cos(x)/1 = 1

applicabilityPatterns: evaluating limits that produce indeterminate forms like 0/0 or infinity/infinity
simplifying complex limit expressions by differentiating numerator and denominator

primitives.name: Newton's Method
primitives.formalStatement: Newton's method for finding roots of f(x) = 0: starting from initial guess x_0, iterate x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n). Under suitable conditions, convergence is quadratic: error at step n+1 is proportional to error^2 at step n.
primitives.computationalForm: x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n). Convergence: if |f(x_0)f''(x_0)| < |f'(x_0)|^2 (roughly). Failure modes: f'(x_n) = 0, cycling, divergence from poor initial guess.

dependencies.description: Newton's method uses the derivative to approximate the root

compositionRules.yields: Iterative root-finding using tangent line approximation
conditions: When a closed-form solution to f(x)=0 is unavailable

applicabilityPatterns: numerically finding zeros of equations that cannot be solved algebraically
rapidly improving an approximate solution to an equation

primitives.name: Implicit Differentiation
primitives.formalStatement: When y is defined implicitly by an equation F(x,y) = 0, differentiate both sides with respect to x, treating y as a function of x (applying chain rule): dF/dx + (dF/dy)(dy/dx) = 0, solving for dy/dx.
primitives.computationalForm: Given F(x,y)=0: dy/dx = -(dF/dx)/(dF/dy). Example: x^2 + y^2 = 1, differentiate: 2x + 2y*dy/dx = 0, so dy/dx = -x/y.

dependencies.description: Implicit differentiation requires the chain rule to differentiate y(x)
Core differentiation concept

compositionRules.yields: Differentiation of curves defined by equations rather than explicit functions
conditions: When y cannot be easily solved for explicitly
compositionRules.example: Circle x^2+y^2=r^2: dy/dx = -x/y without needing y = +/-sqrt(r^2-x^2)

applicabilityPatterns: finding derivatives when the dependent variable is not explicitly isolated
differentiating relations defined by equations involving both variables

primitives.name: Derivatives of Trigonometric Functions
primitives.formalStatement: d/dx sin(x) = cos(x). d/dx cos(x) = -sin(x). d/dx tan(x) = sec^2(x). d/dx sec(x) = sec(x)tan(x). d/dx csc(x) = -csc(x)cot(x). d/dx cot(x) = -csc^2(x).
primitives.computationalForm: sin' = cos, cos' = -sin, tan' = sec^2, sec' = sec*tan. With chain rule: d/dx sin(f(x)) = cos(f(x))*f'(x).

dependencies.description: These are derivatives of specific functions
Combined with chain rule for composite trig functions
Deriving the derivative of sine
Deriving the derivative of cosine

compositionRules.yields: Derivatives of composite trig functions: d/dx sin(g(x)) = cos(g(x))*g'(x)
conditions: When differentiating trigonometric functions of other expressions
compositionRules.example: d/dx sin(x^2) = 2x*cos(x^2)

applicabilityPatterns: differentiating expressions involving sine, cosine, and other trig functions
computing velocity of objects in circular or oscillatory motion

primitives.name: Riemann Sum
primitives.formalStatement: A Riemann sum approximates the integral of f over [a,b] by partitioning into n subintervals and summing: S_n = sum_{i=1}^{n} f(x_i*) * Delta_x_i, where x_i* is a sample point in each subinterval.
primitives.computationalForm: Left sum: S_n = sum_{i=0}^{n-1} f(a + i*h)*h. Right sum: S_n = sum_{i=1}^{n} f(a + i*h)*h. Midpoint: S_n = sum_{i=0}^{n-1} f(a + (i+0.5)*h)*h. h = (b-a)/n.

dependencies.description: The definite integral is the limit of Riemann sums as n -> infinity
Riemann sums operate on real-valued functions

compositionRules.yields: The definite integral: integral_a^b f(x)dx = lim_{n->inf} sum f(x_i*)*Delta_x
conditions: When defining the integral as the limit of approximating sums
compositionRules.example: Area under y=x^2 from 0 to 1: lim_{n->inf} sum (i/n)^2 * (1/n) = 1/3

applicabilityPatterns: approximating the area under a curve using rectangles
providing the foundation for the definition of the definite integral

primitives.name: Definite Integral
primitives.formalStatement: The definite integral of f from a to b is integral_a^b f(x)dx = lim_{n->inf} sum_{i=1}^{n} f(x_i*)*Delta_x, when this limit exists. It represents the signed area between f and the x-axis over [a,b].
primitives.computationalForm: Properties: integral_a^b c*f = c*integral_a^b f. integral_a^b (f+g) = integral_a^b f + integral_a^b g. integral_a^b f = -integral_b^a f. integral_a^c f = integral_a^b f + integral_b^c f.

dependencies.description: Definite integral is the limit of Riemann sums
The integral is defined as a limit

compositionRules.yields: Practical evaluation: integral_a^b f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a) where F' = f
conditions: When an antiderivative of f is known

applicabilityPatterns: computing the total accumulation of a quantity over an interval
finding the area enclosed between a curve and the x-axis

primitives.name: Antiderivative (Indefinite Integral)
primitives.formalStatement: An antiderivative of f is a function F such that F'(x) = f(x). The indefinite integral integral f(x)dx = F(x) + C represents the family of all antiderivatives, where C is an arbitrary constant.
primitives.computationalForm: integral x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C (n != -1). integral 1/x dx = ln|x| + C. integral e^x dx = e^x + C. integral sin(x)dx = -cos(x) + C.

dependencies.description: Antiderivative is the reverse of differentiation

compositionRules.yields: Inverse relationship: d/dx integral f(x)dx = f(x) and integral f'(x)dx = f(x) + C
conditions: When switching between differentiation and integration
compositionRules.example: d/dx(-cos(x)) = sin(x), so integral sin(x)dx = -cos(x) + C

applicabilityPatterns: finding a function whose derivative is a given function
reversing differentiation to recover original functions

primitives.name: Improper Integral
primitives.formalStatement: An improper integral is a definite integral with an infinite limit or a discontinuous integrand. integral_a^inf f(x)dx = lim_{b->inf} integral_a^b f(x)dx. If the limit exists and is finite, the integral converges.
primitives.computationalForm: integral_1^inf 1/x^p dx: converges to 1/(p-1) if p > 1, diverges if p <= 1. integral_0^1 1/x^p dx: converges to 1/(1-p) if p < 1, diverges if p >= 1.

dependencies.description: Improper integral extends the definite integral to infinite domains
Improper integrals are defined as limits

compositionRules.yields: Integral test for series: sum 1/n diverges because integral 1/x dx diverges
conditions: When using integrals to test convergence of series
compositionRules.example: integral_1^inf 1/x^2 dx = 1 (converges), so sum 1/n^2 converges (to pi^2/6)

applicabilityPatterns: computing the total area under a curve extending to infinity
determining whether an infinite sum converges using the integral test

primitives.name: Area Under a Curve
primitives.formalStatement: The area between the curve y = f(x), the x-axis, and the vertical lines x = a and x = b is given by integral_a^b |f(x)| dx. When f(x) >= 0, this equals integral_a^b f(x) dx.
primitives.computationalForm: Area = integral_a^b |f(x)| dx. Between two curves: integral_a^b |f(x) - g(x)| dx = integral_a^b [upper - lower] dx.

dependencies.description: Area is the geometric interpretation of the definite integral

compositionRules.yields: Geometric interpretation: the integral computes signed area; absolute value gives true area
conditions: When computing the area enclosed by curves
compositionRules.example: Area between y=x^2 and y=x from 0 to 1: integral_0^1 (x - x^2)dx = 1/6

applicabilityPatterns: computing the area enclosed between curves
finding the total area of a region bounded by a function and the axes

primitives.name: Arc Length
primitives.formalStatement: The arc length of the curve y = f(x) from x = a to x = b is L = integral_a^b sqrt(1 + [f'(x)]^2) dx. This follows from the infinitesimal Pythagorean theorem: ds^2 = dx^2 + dy^2.
primitives.computationalForm: L = integral_a^b sqrt(1 + (dy/dx)^2) dx. Parametric: L = integral_a^b sqrt((dx/dt)^2 + (dy/dt)^2) dt. Polar: L = integral sqrt(r^2 + (dr/d_theta)^2) d_theta.

dependencies.description: Arc length is computed as a definite integral
The infinitesimal arc length formula uses the Pythagorean theorem
The integrand involves the derivative f'(x)

compositionRules.yields: Infinitesimal distance: ds = sqrt(dx^2+dy^2), the Pythagorean theorem applied at infinitesimal scale
conditions: When computing the length of a curved path
compositionRules.example: Arc length of y=x^(3/2) from 0 to 4: integral_0^4 sqrt(1 + (3/2*sqrt(x))^2) dx

applicabilityPatterns: computing the length of a curve in the plane
finding the distance traveled along a curved path

primitives.name: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 1
primitives.formalStatement: If f is continuous on [a,b], then the function F(x) = integral_a^x f(t)dt is continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), and F'(x) = f(x). Differentiation undoes integration.
primitives.computationalForm: d/dx integral_a^x f(t) dt = f(x). With chain rule: d/dx integral_a^{g(x)} f(t) dt = f(g(x)) * g'(x).

dependencies.description: FTC1 relates the definite integral to differentiation
Continuity of f is required for FTC1
FTC1 states that the derivative of the integral function is f

compositionRules.yields: d/dx integral_a^{g(x)} f(t)dt = f(g(x))*g'(x): differentiating integrals with variable upper limits
conditions: When the upper limit of integration is a function of x
compositionRules.example: d/dx integral_0^{x^2} sin(t)dt = sin(x^2)*2x

applicabilityPatterns: differentiating an integral with a variable upper limit
proving that every continuous function has an antiderivative

primitives.name: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 2
primitives.formalStatement: If f is continuous on [a,b] and F is any antiderivative of f (F' = f), then integral_a^b f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a). Integration is evaluated using antiderivatives.
primitives.computationalForm: integral_a^b f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a) = [F(x)]_a^b. Example: integral_0^pi sin(x)dx = [-cos(x)]_0^pi = -cos(pi)-(-cos(0)) = 1+1 = 2.

dependencies.description: FTC2 is the practical evaluation consequence of FTC1
FTC2 uses antiderivatives to evaluate integrals

compositionRules.yields: Practical integration: find F with F'=f, then integral = F(b)-F(a)
conditions: When an antiderivative can be found
compositionRules.example: integral_1^e 1/x dx = [ln(x)]_1^e = ln(e) - ln(1) = 1

applicabilityPatterns: evaluating definite integrals using antiderivatives instead of Riemann sums
computing exact areas, volumes, and accumulations

primitives.name: Integration by Parts
primitives.formalStatement: integral u dv = uv - integral v du. Equivalently, integral_a^b f(x)g'(x)dx = [f(x)g(x)]_a^b - integral_a^b f'(x)g(x)dx. This is the integration analog of the product rule.
primitives.computationalForm: Choose u and dv (LIATE rule: Log, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential — u from left). integral u dv = uv - integral v du. May need multiple applications.

dependencies.description: Integration by parts is derived from the product rule
Finding the antiderivative v from dv
Used to evaluate the resulting integral

compositionRules.yields: Complete integration toolkit: substitution for chain-rule integrands, parts for product integrands
conditions: When neither direct antiderivative nor simple substitution works
compositionRules.example: integral x*e^x dx: u=x, dv=e^x dx, yields x*e^x - integral e^x dx = x*e^x - e^x + C

applicabilityPatterns: integrating products of functions where one simplifies upon differentiation
reducing complex integrals to simpler forms

primitives.name: Substitution Rule (u-substitution)
primitives.formalStatement: If u = g(x) is differentiable, then integral f(g(x))*g'(x)dx = integral f(u)du. This is the integration counterpart of the chain rule.
primitives.computationalForm: Set u = g(x), du = g'(x)dx. Substitute: integral f(g(x))g'(x)dx = integral f(u)du. For definite integrals: change limits to u(a) and u(b).

dependencies.description: u-substitution is the reverse of the chain rule
Substitution simplifies finding antiderivatives

compositionRules.yields: Combined integration strategy: try substitution first, then parts, then partial fractions
conditions: When working through integration strategy
compositionRules.example: integral 2x*cos(x^2)dx: u=x^2, du=2x dx, = integral cos(u)du = sin(u)+C = sin(x^2)+C

applicabilityPatterns: integrating composite functions where the inner function's derivative is present
simplifying integrals by changing variables

primitives.name: Trapezoidal Rule
primitives.formalStatement: The trapezoidal rule approximates integral_a^b f(x)dx by summing trapezoid areas: T_n = h/2 * [f(x_0) + 2*f(x_1) + 2*f(x_2) + ... + 2*f(x_{n-1}) + f(x_n)] where h = (b-a)/n.

dependencies.description: Trapezoidal rule numerically approximates definite integrals
Trapezoidal rule improves on Riemann sum by using trapezoids instead of rectangles

compositionRules.yields: Hierarchy of numerical integration methods: midpoint O(h^2), trapezoidal O(h^2), Simpson's O(h^4)
conditions: When choosing between numerical integration methods
compositionRules.example: integral_0^1 e^x dx with n=4: T_4 ~ 1.7189, exact = e-1 ~ 1.7183

applicabilityPatterns: numerically approximating integrals when antiderivatives are unknown or complex
computing integrals from tabulated data points

primitives.name: Simpson's Rule
primitives.formalStatement: Simpson's rule approximates integral_a^b f(x)dx using parabolic arcs: S_n = h/3 * [f(x_0) + 4f(x_1) + 2f(x_2) + 4f(x_3) + ... + 4f(x_{n-1}) + f(x_n)] where n is even and h = (b-a)/n.
primitives.computationalForm: S_n = (h/3)*[f(a) + 4*sum_{odd} f(x_i) + 2*sum_{even} f(x_i) + f(b)]. Error: |E_S| <= (b-a)^5/(180*n^4) * max|f^(4)(x)|. O(h^4) accuracy.

dependencies.description: Simpson's rule improves on the trapezoidal rule with higher-order approximation

compositionRules.yields: Adaptive quadrature: use Simpson's on fine intervals, trapezoidal where the function is linear
conditions: When high accuracy is needed for smooth integrands
compositionRules.example: integral_0^1 e^x dx with n=4: S_4 ~ 1.71828, exact ~ 1.71828 (4+ correct decimals)

applicabilityPatterns: achieving high-accuracy numerical integration with fewer function evaluations
integrating smooth functions where the error bound must be small

primitives.name: Partial Fractions
primitives.formalStatement: Any proper rational function P(x)/Q(x) can be decomposed into a sum of simpler fractions with linear or irreducible quadratic denominators, enabling integration term by term.

dependencies.description: Partial fractions enable finding antiderivatives of rational functions

compositionRules.yields: Rational function integration: partial fractions decompose, then ln or arctan integrate each term
conditions: When integrating a rational function
compositionRules.example: integral 1/(x^2+1) dx = arctan(x) + C (irreducible quadratic case)

applicabilityPatterns: integrating rational functions by decomposing into simpler fractions
computing Laplace transform inverses

primitives.name: Integration Strategy
primitives.formalStatement: A systematic approach to integration: (1) Try direct antiderivative. (2) Try u-substitution. (3) Try integration by parts. (4) Try partial fractions (for rationals). (5) Try trigonometric identities or substitution. (6) Use numerical methods.
primitives.computationalForm: Decision tree: Is it a standard form? -> Direct. Is there a composition? -> u-sub. Product of different types? -> Parts (LIATE). Rational? -> Partial fractions. Trig? -> Identities. Otherwise -> Numerical.

dependencies.description: u-substitution is a core integration strategy
Integration by parts is a core integration strategy
Partial fractions handle rational function integrals

compositionRules.yields: Complete practical integration: strategy to evaluate any definite integral
conditions: When facing an integral that does not have an obvious antiderivative
compositionRules.example: integral x*ln(x) dx: not direct, not substitution, try parts: u=ln(x), dv=x dx

applicabilityPatterns: choosing the right integration method for a given integrand
systematically working through difficult integrals

primitives.name: Ordinary Differential Equation
primitives.formalStatement: An ordinary differential equation (ODE) is an equation involving a function y(x) and its derivatives: F(x, y, y', y'', ..., y^(n)) = 0. The order is the highest derivative present. A solution is a function that satisfies the equation on an interval.
primitives.computationalForm: First order: y' = f(x,y). Second order: y'' = f(x,y,y'). General solution contains n arbitrary constants for nth-order ODE. Particular solution: fix constants via initial/boundary conditions.

dependencies.description: ODEs are equations involving derivatives
Higher-order ODEs involve second and higher derivatives

compositionRules.yields: Complete ODE problem: equation + initial conditions = unique solution (when existence/uniqueness holds)
conditions: When modeling a physical system's evolution from a known starting state
compositionRules.example: dy/dx = 2x, y(0) = 1: solution y = x^2 + 1

applicabilityPatterns: modeling systems where the rate of change depends on the current state
describing physical laws relating a quantity to its derivatives

primitives.name: Initial Value Problem
primitives.formalStatement: An initial value problem (IVP) is an ODE together with initial conditions: y^(k)(x_0) = y_k for k = 0, 1, ..., n-1. For a first-order IVP: y' = f(x,y), y(x_0) = y_0.
primitives.computationalForm: Solve IVP: (1) Find general solution of ODE. (2) Apply initial conditions to determine constants. Example: y' = 2x, y(0)=1 -> y = x^2 + C, C=1 -> y = x^2 + 1.

dependencies.description: An IVP is an ODE with initial conditions

compositionRules.yields: Guaranteed unique solution: under Picard-Lindelof conditions, the IVP has exactly one solution
conditions: When the ODE satisfies Lipschitz continuity conditions
compositionRules.example: y' = y, y(0)=1: unique solution y = e^x (exponential growth)

applicabilityPatterns: predicting the future state of a system from its current state and rate law
solving physical problems with known starting conditions

primitives.name: Taylor Series
primitives.formalStatement: The Taylor series of f about x = a is f(x) = sum_{n=0}^{infinity} f^(n)(a)/n! * (x-a)^n = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a) + f''(a)(x-a)^2/2! + ... The series converges to f(x) within the radius of convergence.

dependencies.description: Taylor coefficients are computed from nth derivatives

compositionRules.yields: Local polynomial approximation: truncate Taylor series to get nth degree polynomial near x=a
conditions: When approximating a function near a point with a polynomial
compositionRules.example: sin(x) ~ x - x^3/6 + x^5/120 near x=0 (5th degree approximation)

applicabilityPatterns: approximating functions with polynomials for computation
analyzing local behavior of functions near a point

primitives.name: Maclaurin Series
primitives.formalStatement: A Maclaurin series is a Taylor series centered at a = 0: f(x) = sum_{n=0}^{infinity} f^(n)(0)/n! * x^n. It is the special case of Taylor series at the origin.
primitives.computationalForm: Key Maclaurin series: e^x = sum x^n/n!, sin(x) = sum (-1)^n x^(2n+1)/(2n+1)!, cos(x) = sum (-1)^n x^(2n)/(2n)!, ln(1+x) = sum (-1)^(n+1) x^n/n for |x|<=1.

dependencies.description: Maclaurin series is Taylor series at a=0

compositionRules.yields: Proof of Euler's formula: e^(ix) = cos(x) + i*sin(x) by comparing Maclaurin series of both sides
conditions: When connecting exponential and trigonometric functions via series

applicabilityPatterns: computing standard function values using series expansions at x=0
proving identities by comparing series term by term

primitives.name: Harmonic Oscillator ODE
primitives.formalStatement: The harmonic oscillator equation is d^2x/dt^2 + omega^2*x = 0. The general solution is x(t) = A*cos(omega*t) + B*sin(omega*t) = C*cos(omega*t + phi). Damped: d^2x/dt^2 + 2*zeta*omega*dx/dt + omega^2*x = 0.
primitives.computationalForm: Undamped: x(t) = A*cos(omega*t+phi), omega = sqrt(k/m). Damped: x(t) = e^(-zeta*omega*t)[A*cos(omega_d*t) + B*sin(omega_d*t)] where omega_d = omega*sqrt(1-zeta^2).

dependencies.description: The harmonic oscillator is a second-order ODE
The harmonic oscillator ODE is the mathematical formulation of SHM

compositionRules.yields: Driven harmonic oscillator: d^2x/dt^2 + 2*zeta*omega*dx/dt + omega^2*x = F_0*cos(omega_d*t), with resonance analysis
conditions: When modeling a system driven by an external periodic force
compositionRules.example: Mass-spring-dashpot: resonance when driving frequency matches natural frequency

applicabilityPatterns: modeling oscillatory systems like springs, pendulums, and LC circuits
analyzing damped vibrations and their decay rates

primitives.name: Optimization (Extrema)
primitives.formalStatement: A local minimum (maximum) of f occurs at x=c if f(c) <= f(x) (f(c) >= f(x)) for all x in some neighborhood of c. A global extremum is the absolute minimum or maximum on the entire domain.
primitives.computationalForm: Find extrema: (1) Find critical points (f'=0 or undefined). (2) Classify using first or second derivative test. (3) Compare with endpoint values for closed intervals. Global: compare all critical and endpoint values.

dependencies.description: Extrema occur at critical points or endpoints
Used to classify extrema
Alternative classification of extrema

compositionRules.yields: Constrained optimization: finding extrema subject to equality constraints
conditions: When optimizing a function subject to constraints
compositionRules.example: Maximize area of rectangle with fixed perimeter: A=xy, constraint: 2x+2y=P

applicabilityPatterns: finding the maximum or minimum value of a function
solving word problems that ask for the best, largest, smallest, or most efficient quantity

primitives.name: Taylor's Theorem with Remainder
primitives.formalStatement: If f has (n+1) continuous derivatives on an interval containing a, then f(x) = P_n(x) + R_n(x), where P_n is the nth Taylor polynomial and R_n(x) = f^(n+1)(c)/(n+1)! * (x-a)^(n+1) for some c between a and x (Lagrange form).
primitives.computationalForm: |R_n(x)| <= M * |x-a|^(n+1) / (n+1)! where M = max|f^(n+1)(t)| on [a,x]. This bounds the approximation error of the nth Taylor polynomial.

dependencies.description: Remainder theorem quantifies the error of Taylor polynomial approximation
Error bound involves the (n+1)th derivative

compositionRules.yields: Certified approximation: Taylor polynomial plus error bound gives guaranteed accuracy
conditions: When needing to know how accurate a Taylor approximation is

applicabilityPatterns: bounding the error of polynomial approximation to a function
determining how many Taylor terms are needed for a given accuracy

primitives.name: Existence and Uniqueness Theorem (Picard-Lindelof)
primitives.formalStatement: If f(x,y) and df/dy are continuous in a rectangle containing (x_0, y_0), then the IVP y' = f(x,y), y(x_0) = y_0 has a unique solution in some interval around x_0.
primitives.computationalForm: Check conditions: f continuous => existence (Peano). f and df/dy continuous (Lipschitz in y) => uniqueness. Counter-example: y' = y^(2/3), y(0)=0 has multiple solutions (f not Lipschitz).

dependencies.description: The theorem guarantees existence and uniqueness for IVPs
Continuity conditions are the hypotheses

compositionRules.yields: Confidence that physical ODE models have well-defined solutions under reasonable conditions
conditions: When verifying that an ODE problem is well-posed
compositionRules.example: y' = sin(xy), y(0)=1: f=sin(xy) and df/dy = x*cos(xy) are continuous everywhere, so unique solution exists

applicabilityPatterns: verifying that a differential equation model has a well-defined unique solution
identifying pathological ODEs that may have non-unique solutions

primitives.name: First Derivative Test
primitives.formalStatement: If f'(x) changes from positive to negative at critical point c, then f has a local maximum at c. If f'(x) changes from negative to positive at c, then f has a local minimum at c. If f' does not change sign, c is neither.
primitives.computationalForm: At critical point c: check sign of f'(x) for x < c and x > c. +/- => local max. -/+ => local min. +/+ or -/- => neither (inflection or saddle).

dependencies.description: First derivative test classifies critical points
Test uses the sign of the first derivative

compositionRules.yields: Two complementary methods for classifying critical points: sign change vs concavity
conditions: When classifying critical points of differentiable functions
compositionRules.example: f(x) = x^4: f'(0) = 0, f' changes from - to +, local minimum (second derivative test: f''(0)=0, inconclusive)

applicabilityPatterns: determining whether a critical point is a local maximum, minimum, or neither
analyzing the increasing/decreasing behavior of functions

primitives.name: Second Derivative Test
primitives.formalStatement: If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) > 0, then f has a local minimum at c. If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) < 0, then f has a local maximum at c. If f''(c) = 0, the test is inconclusive.
primitives.computationalForm: At critical point c: compute f''(c). f''(c) > 0 => concave up => local min. f''(c) < 0 => concave down => local max. f''(c) = 0 => use first derivative test instead.

dependencies.description: Applies to critical points where f'=0
Uses the second derivative for classification

compositionRules.yields: Complete critical point classification toolkit
conditions: When classifying extrema efficiently

applicabilityPatterns: quickly classifying critical points when the second derivative is easy to compute
determining concavity at critical points

primitives.name: Euler's Method
primitives.formalStatement: Euler's method numerically solves y' = f(x,y), y(x_0) = y_0 by iterating: y_{n+1} = y_n + h*f(x_n, y_n), x_{n+1} = x_n + h, where h is the step size. Convergence is first-order: global error O(h).
primitives.computationalForm: y_{n+1} = y_n + h*f(x_n, y_n). Local truncation error: O(h^2). Global error: O(h). Improved Euler (Heun): y* = y_n + h*f(x_n,y_n), y_{n+1} = y_n + (h/2)*[f(x_n,y_n) + f(x_{n+1},y*)].

dependencies.description: Euler's method numerically solves initial value problems
Uses the derivative (slope) to step forward

compositionRules.yields: Suite of iterative numerical methods: Euler for ODEs, Newton for root-finding
conditions: When comparing numerical approaches to different mathematical problems

applicabilityPatterns: numerically solving ODEs when analytical solutions are unavailable
simulating dynamical systems on a computer

primitives.name: Gradient Descent (Preview)
primitives.formalStatement: Gradient descent iteratively minimizes a function f by stepping in the direction of steepest descent: x_{n+1} = x_n - alpha * f'(x_n) in 1D, or x_{n+1} = x_n - alpha * grad(f)(x_n) in multiple dimensions. alpha is the learning rate.
primitives.computationalForm: x_{n+1} = x_n - alpha * f'(x_n). Choose alpha small enough for convergence. Convergence criterion: |f'(x_n)| < epsilon. Multi-dimensional: x_{n+1} = x_n - alpha * [df/dx_1, df/dx_2, ..., df/dx_n]^T.

dependencies.description: Gradient descent is an iterative optimization algorithm
Uses the derivative (gradient) to determine the descent direction
Both are iterative methods; gradient descent trades convergence speed for simplicity

compositionRules.yields: Optimization by following the negative gradient: always step toward lower function values
conditions: When finding the minimum of a differentiable function iteratively

applicabilityPatterns: iteratively finding the minimum of a function when no closed-form solution exists
training machine learning models by minimizing loss functions

primitives.name: Separation of Variables for ODEs
primitives.formalStatement: For a separable ODE dy/dx = f(x)*g(y): rewrite as dy/g(y) = f(x)dx, then integrate both sides. This yields integral dy/g(y) = integral f(x)dx + C.
primitives.computationalForm: dy/dx = f(x)*g(y) -> integral 1/g(y) dy = integral f(x) dx + C. Example: dy/dx = xy -> integral dy/y = integral x dx -> ln|y| = x^2/2 + C -> y = Ae^(x^2/2).

dependencies.description: Separation of variables solves a class of first-order ODEs
Both sides are solved by finding antiderivatives

compositionRules.yields: Direct solution of separable ODEs by integrating both sides
conditions: When the ODE can be written as g(y)dy = f(x)dx
compositionRules.example: dy/dx = -y/x -> integral dy/y = -integral dx/x -> ln|y| = -ln|x| + C -> y = A/x

applicabilityPatterns: solving ODEs where variables can be separated to opposite sides of the equation
modeling exponential growth/decay, logistic growth, and other separable dynamics

primitives.name: Lagrange Multipliers
primitives.formalStatement: To optimize f(x,y) subject to constraint g(x,y) = 0: solve the system grad(f) = lambda * grad(g) and g = 0. The Lagrange multiplier lambda gives the rate of change of the optimal value with respect to the constraint.
primitives.computationalForm: System: df/dx = lambda*dg/dx, df/dy = lambda*dg/dy, g(x,y) = 0. Three equations in three unknowns (x, y, lambda). Extension to multiple constraints: grad(f) = sum lambda_i * grad(g_i).

dependencies.description: Lagrange multipliers solve constrained optimization problems
The method requires computing partial derivatives

compositionRules.yields: Constrained optimization: finding extrema on the constraint surface
conditions: When optimizing a function subject to equality constraints
compositionRules.example: Maximize f(x,y)=xy subject to x+y=10: grad(xy)=lambda*grad(x+y) gives (y,x)=lambda*(1,1), so x=y=5

applicabilityPatterns: optimizing a function subject to equality constraints
finding the extrema of a function restricted to a surface or curve

primitives.name: Laplace Transform Basic Pairs
primitives.formalStatement: The Laplace transform L{f(t)} = integral_0^inf e^(-st)*f(t)dt maps time-domain functions to the s-domain, converting differential equations to algebraic equations. Key pairs: L{1} = 1/s, L{e^(at)} = 1/(s-a), L{sin(bt)} = b/(s^2+b^2), L{cos(bt)} = s/(s^2+b^2).
primitives.computationalForm: L{f'(t)} = s*F(s) - f(0). L{f''(t)} = s^2*F(s) - s*f(0) - f'(0). Inverse: L^{-1}{1/(s-a)} = e^(at). Linearity: L{af+bg} = aF+bG.

dependencies.description: Laplace transforms convert ODEs to algebraic equations
The Laplace transform is defined as an improper integral

compositionRules.yields: Algebraic ODE solution: transform ODE -> solve algebraically in s-domain -> inverse transform back to t-domain
conditions: When solving linear ODEs with constant coefficients and initial conditions

applicabilityPatterns: solving linear ODEs with constant coefficients using algebraic methods
analyzing linear systems using transfer functions
